authors.voice_note: Experienced aussie owner, hikes regularly around San Diego. Slightly competitive about her dog's training. Knows what she's doing but still hits walls.
New aussie owner, slightly overwhelmed, researches everything. Earnest and genuine. Asks the questions every new owner has but is too embarrassed to google.
Casual, warm, works from home. Biscuit has become an unofficial coworker. More observational than advice-seeking.
Experienced, trainer-minded. Practical and direct. Has owned aussies before and knows the breed well. Gets straight to the point.
Thoughtful and sincere. Deals with some health challenges with Wren. Shares personal moments without oversharing.
Ocean Beach local, very active outdoorsy lifestyle. Writes casually and briefly. Koda gets a ton of outdoor time and Chris wants to find a crew.

posts.title: Took Rigby to Dog Beach for the first time
posts.content_text:
Rigby absolutely lost his mind at Dog Beach. First five minutes he just stood at the water's edge barking at the waves like he was personally offended by them. Then some golden retriever ran past and he was off.

Spent the whole time doing laps between the water and the sand. He slept for like four hours when we got home, which honestly was the goal.
image_briefs: Australian Shepherd running along the shoreline at Dog Beach, ears flat, looking back at camera, wet sand and waves in background

posts.title: Clover started circling my toddler in the yard – herding instinct or something to fix?
posts.content_text:
Clover has started circling my toddler when he runs around outside. No snapping or growling — just nudging him and cutting in front. I know aussies are bred to herd but I genuinely can't tell if this needs correcting early or if it's harmless.

Posting a photo. She looks extremely focused and has no idea she's doing anything unusual.
image_briefs: Australian Shepherd puppy standing attentively in a suburban backyard near a small child's legs, looking up with intense focus

posts.title: 'place' was one of the better things we taught Pepper early on
posts.content_text:
One thing I wish I had worked on sooner with Pepper was really proofing the 'place' command. Guests at the door, doorbell going off, just needing five minutes of quiet — having a dog who genuinely wants to go there makes a real difference.

We started with very short durations and built up slowly. One of the more useful things we put time into in year one.

posts.title: Aussie run at OB Dog Beach – Saturday morning
posts.content_text:
Taking Koda down to OB Dog Beach this Saturday around 8am if anyone wants to bring their aussie or herding mix. Usually pretty chill at that hour before it fills up. We'll be near the south end.

No pressure, just throwing it out there.
image_briefs: Australian Shepherd standing at the water's edge of an off-leash beach, looking out toward the ocean, warm morning light

posts.title: Biscuit decided my WFH setup is his personal nap station
posts.content_text:
I've been working from home most days and at some point Biscuit just fully claimed the spot under my desk. Not asking, just existing there. I'm not complaining — he's warm and stays quiet as long as I'm not on video calls.

The second I unmute he lifts his head like he's about to contribute something to the meeting.

posts.title: Vet flagged possible hip dysplasia at age 3 – anyone been through this?
posts.content_text:
Got some concerning news at our last vet visit. Wren is only three and the X-rays are showing early signs of hip dysplasia. Vet said it's not uncommon in the breed but I wasn't expecting it this young.

Has anyone been through this with their aussie? What does long-term management look like? Any supplements or lifestyle changes that genuinely helped?

posts.title: How do you handle recall near other dogs?
posts.content_text:
Rigby has great recall in the backyard and at quieter parks, but the second there are other dogs around it's like I don't exist. I've tried high value treats, I've tried the long line, nothing seems to stick when he's in that zone.

Anyone else dealt with this? What finally worked?

posts.title: How much exercise is too much for a 5-month-old aussie?
posts.content_text:
We got Clover about two months ago and I've been reading conflicting stuff about exercise limits at this age. Some sources say 5 minutes per month of age, others suggest aussies need way more or they go sideways.

She seems totally fine but I don't want to cause joint issues. What were you all actually doing at 5 months?

posts.title: Puzzle feeders actually helped with Rigby's zoomies
posts.content_text:
I was skeptical but adding a puzzle feeder to Rigby's morning routine made a noticeable difference in his overall settleability. He still needs physical exercise but the mental drain from working for his food takes some of the edge off.

We've been doing it about six weeks and the random indoor zoomies have dropped a lot. Not a fix for everything but it helped us.

posts.title: rotating toys sounds obvious but it actually made a difference for Pepper
posts.content_text:
Pepper loses interest in toys within 48 hours if they are always out. I rotate now — about 5 available at a time, swap every few days. Toys she has had for months feel interesting again.

Same idea with puzzle feeders. Novelty matters more than I expected. Does not cost anything extra.
image_briefs: Assortment of dog toys sorted into small groups on a living room rug, organized neatly like they're ready to be rotated out

posts.title: Frozen lick mats changed crate training for us
posts.content_text:
Clover was not into the crate at first. Like, at all. Someone in an aussie group suggested frozen lick mats — I used plain peanut butter and a bit of banana — and it genuinely shifted things within about a week.

She now goes in on her own sometimes when she's tired. I know every dog is different but if you're in crate training hell right now, try it.

posts.title: Best brush for double coat shedding season?
posts.content_text:
Biscuit is blowing his coat right now and I am losing my mind. I've tried a slicker brush, a deshedding rake, and a Furminator, and somehow there is still hair on every surface in my apartment.

Is there a brush that actually makes a visible difference? Or is this just the reality I need to accept for the next six weeks?

posts.title: Pepper shifts into this whole watchful mode when strangers come inside
posts.content_text:
Pepper is completely fine with people outside — parks, the street, anywhere public. But put a stranger inside the house and she is in a different gear. Not aggressive, just very switched on. She will bark once or twice and then quietly keep tabs.

She is four so I do not think this is going away on its own. Is this just an aussie trait or is there something worth actually working on?

posts.title: Morning hike at Mission Trails with Wren
posts.content_text:
We did the Visitor Center loop at Mission Trails this morning, got there early enough that it was still cool out. Wren was in absolute heaven — so many smells, so much terrain to process.

She tried to herd a jogger at the turnaround point, which was mortifying, but he laughed it off. We'll probably be back next weekend.
image_briefs: Australian Shepherd on a dry dirt trail in a Southern California park, on leash, looking off into brushy terrain with alert ears

posts.title: Anyone run Fiesta Island with their aussie?
posts.content_text:
Koda and I go to Fiesta Island a couple mornings a week and I've been meaning to find other aussie owners to run with. Flat, open, great for off-leash if you haven't been.

We usually go Tuesday and Thursday around 7:30. Hit me up if you're interested.

authors.voice_note: Experienced dachshund owner, very health-conscious about IVDD and back care. Gives practical but cautious advice. Earnest and a little intense about breed health.
Casual and a bit funny, newer owner. Frank is a chaos agent. Derek writes like he's still adjusting to having a dachshund and finding it both stressful and hilarious.
Younger owner, attentive, Bao is her first dog. Thoughtful about training and socialization. Writes with a gentle curiosity.
No-nonsense, practical, this is his third dachshund. Gives blunt, experienced advice. Doesn't sugarcoat. Has seen it all.
San Diego local, social, goes to dog beach regularly. Enthusiastic and community-oriented. Mochi is small and a little dramatic.
Older guy, second dachshund, Señor is seven and slowing down a bit. Warm and observational. Writes with a quiet affection for the breed.
Brand new dachshund owner, 14-week-old puppy, slightly overwhelmed. Asks the panicked questions every new owner has but is too deep in it to Google properly.

posts.title: Is my dachshund puppy normal or just feral
posts.content_text:
Pickles is 14 weeks old and she is absolutely unhinged. Bites everything, full zoomies at 11pm, cries the second I leave the room, digs at the carpet like she's searching for buried treasure.

Is this just puppy behavior or is there something specifically dachshund about this? Please tell me this calms down. Posting a photo because words alone don't do it justice.
image_briefs: Dachshund puppy sitting in the middle of a partially chewed dog bed, looking slightly unhinged, mouth open

posts.title: Frank has claimed the couch arm as his surveillance tower
posts.content_text:
Frank is somehow always exactly 10% taller than he needs to be. He's started using the couch arm as a watchtower — just perched up there monitoring the street like a gargoyle. I've tried moving him. He just gets back up.

I've fully accepted this is his spot now. He looks completely ridiculous but honestly the commitment is kind of impressive.
image_briefs: Miniature dachshund perched on the arm of a living room couch, looking out a window with a very serious expression

posts.title: Ramps over stairs – I waited too long and regret it
posts.content_text:
I spent way too long convincing myself Pretzel was fine jumping off the couch. She's small, how bad could it be? Turns out pretty bad. IVDD is real and even low-impact repetitive jumping adds up over years.

We have ramps to the couch and bed now. She figured both out in a few days with treat luring. I just wish I'd done it sooner instead of waiting until I got nervous.

posts.title: Small dog beach morning at OB – anyone in?
posts.content_text:
Thinking about putting together a small dog meetup at OB Dog Beach next Sunday around 9am. It can get overwhelming for little guys when big dogs are running full speed, so I'd love to find a small crew of dachshund or small breed owners to go with.

Comment if you're interested and I'll share more details.
image_briefs: Group of small dogs running on wet sand at an off-leash dog beach, owners standing in the background chatting

posts.title: dachshunds operate on their own timeline – three dogs in and I still remind myself of this
posts.content_text:
Trudy is my third dachshund. They are not like labs or goldens. They understand what you want — they just do not always feel like doing it right now. That is not exactly stubbornness, it is just the breed.

Short sessions, high value treats, a lot of patience. If you are coming from a more biddable breed this is a real adjustment, but it works.

posts.title: Bao gets nervous around bigger dogs – is slow exposure the right approach?
posts.content_text:
Bao is totally fine with other small dogs but around anything medium or larger she gets stiff, sometimes barks, sometimes just tries to hide behind me. She's not aggressive — just clearly uncomfortable.

I've been doing slow, neutral-ground exposure and there's some progress but it's really slow going. Is there a more specific approach that worked for your small dog?

posts.title: Pretzel vs. the backyard sprinklers
posts.content_text:
The backyard sprinklers came on while Pretzel was outside this morning and she absolutely lost it. Full sprint back to the door, sat there glaring at the yard, would not go back out until I walked out first and showed her it was done.

She is four and a half years old. She has never once been harmed by water in her entire life. Absolutely unhinged dog.

posts.title: Señor started resource guarding his bed – how to handle this in an older dog?
posts.content_text:
Señor is seven and has never done this before. In the last month he's started growling if my other dog gets near his sleeping spot. They've coexisted fine for three years and nothing in the house has changed.

I'm not panicking but I want to address it before it escalates. Anyone dealt with sudden onset resource guarding in an older dog?

posts.title: Anyone have a vet rec for dachshund back issues in SD?
posts.content_text:
Pretzel has been showing some stiffness in her back legs after longer walks — nothing dramatic, she's still moving fine, but I want to be proactive before it becomes something. Our regular vet is great but I'm wondering if there's a specialist with more dachshund or IVDD experience in the area.

Mission Valley or North Park would be ideal but I'll travel. Any referrals appreciated.

posts.title: Is Frank's barking ever going to calm down?
posts.content_text:
Frank barks at everything. The mailman, delivery trucks, leaves blowing past the window, me when I take too long getting his food ready. He's 18 months now and I keep hearing it gets better with age.

I'm not seeing any sign of improvement. Any realistic advice? Or is this just what living with a dachshund is?

posts.title: Switched Bao to a harness a few months ago and won't go back
posts.content_text:
I know the harness-vs-collar thing gets repeated a lot in dachshund spaces but it's worth repeating — the way they pull and jerk on leash can stress the neck and spine over time, and dachshunds are already working with a vulnerable back.

Bao wears the Puppia soft harness and it fits her long body really well. Made the switch and haven't thought twice about it since.

posts.title: Keeping Trudy lean has made a bigger difference than anything else
posts.content_text:
Even a pound or two of extra weight puts real stress on a dachshund's spine. Trudy is nine now and I credit her staying mobile to keeping her at a healthy weight her whole life. Measured food, no table scraps, treats counted toward her daily intake.

Boring stuff but it genuinely matters more for this breed than most.
image_briefs: Dachshund sitting on a scale at a vet office, looking up at the camera with a slightly patient expression

posts.title: Evening walk at Sunset Cliffs – looking for small dog company
posts.content_text:
I take Señor along Sunset Cliffs a couple evenings a week, usually around 6:30. It's a beautiful route and not too hot at that hour. He's a slow walker these days — seven years old, bum knee — but he loves the smells and the people.

If you have a small dog and want some company, let me know.

posts.title: Has swimming actually worked as low-impact exercise for your dachshund?
posts.content_text:
My vet mentioned swimming as a good option for dachshunds managing back issues — keeps them active without the impact. Trudy has never been a water dog though.

Has anyone actually introduced a reluctant dachshund to swimming? Did you use a life jacket? Pool or beach? Curious what the reality was like, especially at an older age.

posts.title: Mochi's first trip to Dog Beach
posts.content_text:
She hated it. Absolutely hated it. First she sat down and refused to walk. Then a golden retriever came over to say hi and she barked until it left. Then she found a piece of seaweed and was obsessed with it for 20 minutes.

Then she fell asleep on my lap on the drive home. It was a disaster and also kind of perfect. We're going back.
image_briefs: Small dachshund standing on wet sand at an off-leash beach, looking uncertain, wind slightly lifting her ears

authors.voice_note: Experienced GSD owner, takes training seriously, slightly intense about it. Knows working dog lines and expects a lot from Ares. Not without humor.
First-time GSD owner, works from home, was not fully prepared for a velcro dog. Charmed by it but also processing. Asks a lot of questions.
Mid-level owner, practical, has a good relationship with Bruno but navigates his guarding instincts with patience. Dry and observational in how she writes.
SD local, experienced GSD owner, goes to Fiesta Island regularly. Direct, helpful, and brief. Knows his breed.
Second GSD, experienced and warm. Originally from France, now in San Diego. Practical but affectionate in tone.
Casual outdoorsy guy, big dog owner, less structured than some. Ghost gets plenty of exercise. Dan is easygoing and writes like it.

posts.title: Ares turned 2 and finally looks like a real dog
posts.content_text:
There's a GSD teenage phase nobody warns you about. Huge ears, long legs, none of it proportional. Ares looked like spare parts for most of his first year.

Hit his second birthday and something clicked. He filled out, his coat got dense, and he finally looks like the dog I thought I was getting. Attaching a photo as proof that it does end.
image_briefs: Two-year-old German Shepherd sitting outdoors looking alert and confident, full coat, well-proportioned

posts.title: Zelda's ears won't both stand up at the same time
posts.content_text:
She's 5 months and one ear is up and one is usually flopped. Sometimes they switch. Sometimes both stand for an hour and then both fold. I've been told this is normal during teething but it's been going on for two months.

Still within the normal range or should I be doing something? Posting a photo because it's also kind of adorable.
image_briefs: German Shepherd puppy with one ear upright and one flopped over, looking directly at camera with wide curious eyes

posts.title: Bruno barks at the door even with people he knows
posts.content_text:
My neighbor comes over once a week and has for two years. Bruno has met her probably 80 times. Every single time she rings the bell he loses it. Once she's inside he's completely calm and friendly — it's just that initial moment at the door.

I've tried having her give treats, I've worked on 'quiet,' nothing has stuck. Is this purely territorial or is there a specific approach that's helped others?

posts.title: GSD meetup at Fiesta Island – Sunday morning
posts.content_text:
Taking Nora to Fiesta Island this Sunday around 8am if any other GSD owners want to meet up. She does well with dogs she's been introduced to gradually so figured a low-key group walk would be a good format. Off-leash area is solid out there.

Lmk if you're coming.
image_briefs: German Shepherd running off-leash on a flat open field near water, tongue out, warm morning light

posts.title: Ares knows every command but decides when he feels like doing them
posts.content_text:
He knows everything we've worked on — sit, down, place, heel, all of it. But there are days, especially when something interesting is happening nearby, where he just deliberates. Like he's doing a cost-benefit analysis on whether to comply.

Anyone else deal with this? How do you handle it without turning every session into a standoff?

posts.title: Ghost at Dog Beach – nervous big dog does okay
posts.content_text:
Ghost is 85 lbs and moves like he knows it. I was unsure how he'd handle Dog Beach — too many variables — but he was actually solid. Slow to warm up, sat near the water for about 20 minutes before deciding to go in, then wouldn't leave.

There's something about watching a big dog figure out ocean waves for the first time that's pretty good.
image_briefs: Large German Shepherd standing chest-deep in ocean surf at an off-leash beach, looking calm and satisfied

posts.title: GSDs learn your schedule – lean into it
posts.content_text:
Ares figured out my morning routine within the first few months and he uses it to organize himself. He knows when my alarm goes off, when walks happen, when things settle down. If I deviate without warning, he gets antsy.

I stopped fighting it and built consistency around it instead. A predictable daily structure reduces their background stress in a way that nothing else quite replicates.

posts.title: Zelda follows me to every room including the bathroom – sweet or something to look at?
posts.content_text:
Zelda follows me everywhere. Bathroom, kitchen, out to the mailbox and back. I work from home and she is within arm's reach at all times unless she is sleeping. It is endearing and also slightly claustrophobic.

Just wondering whether this is standard GSD velcro behavior or whether it edges into anxiety and I should be doing something about it.

posts.title: on-leash intros with Nora are always a whole production – what has helped?
posts.content_text:
Nora is selective with new dogs. She's totally fine with dogs she knows but meeting a stranger on leash means stiffness, posturing, and a few tense minutes before she settles. It usually resolves but it makes any dog park feel like a gamble.

Anyone found an approach that makes first introductions smoother for a GSD?

posts.title: Bruno spent the whole afternoon as a leaning post
posts.content_text:
I have a standing desk and Bruno decided it was his personal resting spot. He spent most of the afternoon with his chin resting on my forearm while I tried to type. Every time I gently moved him he just shifted and moved with me.

Got maybe three focused hours of work in. Not actually complaining. He was warm and somehow I had better posture than usual.
image_briefs: German Shepherd resting his chin on a person's forearm at a standing desk, looking up with soft relaxed eyes

posts.title: Start crate training before you think you need it
posts.content_text:
I skipped crate training with Zelda because she seemed fine without it early on. Then I had to board her. She was completely unprepared and it was stressful for everyone involved.

Doing it now at 10 months and it's going okay but it would have been so much easier at 8 weeks. Just get it out of the way from the start.

posts.title: Two blow-out seasons a year – get ahead of them or regret it
posts.content_text:
Kiefer goes through two major coat dumps a year and if I'm not ready, everything I own turns gray. What's helped: daily brushing in the two weeks before the blow-out peaks, a good undercoat rake (not a Furminator — an actual wide-toothed rake), and one professional deshed bath at the start of each season.

The rest of the year is manageable. Those two windows are not.

posts.title: What are you feeding a large GSD without destroying your budget?
posts.content_text:
Ghost is 85 lbs and we've gone through several kibbles trying to find something that works — coat, digestion, energy. Currently on Purina Pro Plan and it's been fine but I'm curious what other GSD owners are running with and whether anyone's noticed a real difference in coat or energy from switching.

Not specifically looking for raw diet recommendations, just curious what's working.

posts.title: Get hip X-rays before you see any symptoms
posts.content_text:
I have had Nora's hips screened twice so far — no issues, but GSDs are one of the higher-risk breeds and knowing early changes how you manage exercise and weight over time.

A lot of vets will do OFA-style screening starting around 2 years. Worth asking about if it has not come up at your appointments yet.

posts.title: Morning walks at Lake Murray – looking for company
posts.content_text:
I take Kiefer around Lake Murray on Saturday mornings, usually around 7:30 before it gets crowded. Good trail, not too many bikes, nice length for a longer leashed walk.

If you have a GSD or another large dog and want to join occasionally, feel free to reach out.

authors.voice_note: Experienced husky owner, second husky, San Diego local. Has learned every lesson the hard way and is now a pragmatic, useful voice in the community.
Newer owner who got a husky because he's active. Now discovering the gap between 'active' and 'husky active.' Still figuring it out, mostly with humor.
Mid-level husky owner, loves the breed but very upfront about the challenges. Dry sense of humor. Writes like someone who's accepted reality.
Experienced, owns two huskies, gives practical no-fluff advice. Has tried everything and knows what works.
Newer owner who moved to SD from a colder state. Navigating husky life in a warmer climate and dealing with Nova's very loud separation feelings.
Trail runner, outdoorsy, Diesel is his running partner. Brief and practical. Diesel gets a lot of miles.

posts.title: Mako's first experience with snow was not what I expected
posts.content_text:
Drove up to Big Bear last winter and let Mako out into about six inches of snow. I expected some kind of ancestral awakening — a dog coming home. Instead he stood at the edge of it for a full minute, sniffed it, and looked back at me like I'd made an error.

Then he went completely feral. Rolled in it, sprinted in circles, wouldn't get back in the car for 45 minutes. So not an awakening — just chaos. Which is on brand.
image_briefs: Husky rolling joyfully in snow on a mountain hillside, ears back, completely covered in white powder

posts.title: Has anyone actually gotten reliable recall on a husky?
posts.content_text:
Loki has solid recall inside and in the fenced yard. The moment she's in open space — any open space — it's like I don't exist. We've worked on it consistently for two years and I still won't take her off leash anywhere without a fence.

Is this just what huskies are, or is there something specific that's actually worked for people?

posts.title: Sasha talks back – is this just a husky thing?
posts.content_text:
When I tell Sasha no she makes this sound that is genuinely indistinguishable from arguing with me. Long drawn-out vocalizations, direct eye contact. I grew up with labs and I have never experienced anything like this.

Is this normal? Do they all do it? Is there any way to train around it or do I just accept the commentary?

posts.title: Husky play morning at Grape Street – Saturday 8:30
posts.content_text:
Bringing Loki to Grape Street Dog Park this Saturday around 8:30am. She does best with other high-energy dogs and Saturdays early tend to be less chaotic before the crowds arrive.

Would love to meet other husky owners. She is friendly, vaccinated, and very loud.
image_briefs: Husky running at full speed inside a fenced dog park, tongue out, motion blur on background

posts.title: Diesel ran 12 miles and could have kept going
posts.content_text:
Took Diesel on a longer trail run through Cuyamaca State Park — about 12 miles total. I was done at mile 10. He was still pulling forward, still scanning the terrain, still had reserves I didn't.

Got back to the car and he ate his food and looked at me like we'd done a warm-up. I need to either train harder or get a shorter dog.
image_briefs: Husky running alongside a person on a dry mountain trail, fully in stride, happy open-mouth expression

posts.title: Nova howls every time we leave – neighbor left a note
posts.content_text:
Nova doesn't bark — she howls. Full wolf mode the moment the front door closes. Our downstairs neighbor left a note. We've started departure training and I leave a frozen Kong every time, but progress is slow.

Anyone dealt with this specifically? Not sure if it's separation anxiety or just standard husky vocals that I need to train around.

posts.title: The off-leash mistake I made with my first husky
posts.content_text:
Loki is my second husky and I made this mistake with my first one. They will sprint away from you the moment something catches their nose, and recall alone is not going to bring them back. It is not defiance — it is how they are wired.

Long line or fenced area has been our only reliable option. I know some owners have made off-leash work through months of consistent training, but it was not something I was willing to gamble on a second time.

posts.title: Sasha is 14 months and still actively destroying things – at what point does this end?
posts.content_text:
Sasha has destroyed: one throw pillow, three socks, a corner of my IKEA couch, and the rubber weather stripping off my back door. She is 14 months. I have chew toys, puzzle feeders, a flirt pole, and we do at least an hour of exercise a day.

Looking for a realistic timeline here, not just 'be consistent.' At what point does this actually taper off?
image_briefs: Husky sitting in the middle of a destroyed throw pillow with stuffing everywhere, looking completely unrepentant

posts.title: Managing a husky in San Diego heat – what's your summer routine?
posts.content_text:
Mako is completely different once June hits. Refuses to walk after 9am, pants heavily if we're out more than 20 minutes in the heat, basically shuts down until evening. I know huskies aren't built for this climate — I just want to make sure I'm handling it correctly.

What does your summer routine actually look like from June through September?

posts.title: Sasha found the gap in my fence I didn't know existed
posts.content_text:
Came home from work to find my neighbor standing in my driveway holding Sasha by the collar. She'd been in his backyard for an hour. I had no idea there was a loose board at the back of my fence, but Sasha absolutely knew — had clearly been monitoring it and waited for her moment.

The patch is done. I did a full fence audit. She is sitting in the yard right now looking at me like she's already found the next one.

posts.title: Two huskies is actually easier in some ways
posts.content_text:
Got a second husky 18 months ago and people thought I was losing my mind. But Yukon and Kira burn each other out in ways I simply cannot match. They play hard for an hour and both crash.

Solo husky energy is relentless. Two seems chaotic but they self-regulate in ways one dog never will. I sleep better now than I did with just one.

posts.title: Almost shaved Nova for summer heat – glad I didn't
posts.content_text:
A groomer suggested shaving Nova for the summer heat and I almost agreed. Someone talked me out of it. The double coat actually insulates against heat as well as cold — shaving disrupts that and can permanently affect the coat texture.

Keeping it well-brushed so air can move through is what actually helps. Matting is the problem, not the coat itself.

posts.title: How often are you bathing your husky and with what?
posts.content_text:
Yukon has that self-cleaning double coat tendency but he still needs actual baths. We do once a month but I feel like the undercoat doesn't fully release without more regular washing.

Anyone have a specific routine or shampoo that helps with coat management? Bonus if there's something that helps with the smell between baths.

posts.title: Trail run at Los Peñasquitos Canyon – looking for running dogs
posts.content_text:
Taking Diesel through Los Peñasquitos Canyon on Sunday morning, probably 6–8 miles depending on pace. Looking for one or two other owners with dogs that can handle a sustained run — huskies, malamutes, high-energy mixes welcome.

Starting around 7am at the Mercy Road trailhead. Let me know if you're in.

posts.title: Front-clip harness made loose leash walking actually possible
posts.content_text:
Yukon is 65 lbs and used to drag me on every walk. Tried a prong collar, didn't love it. Tried a head halter, he hated it. Ended up on the Ruffwear Front Range and the difference in the first week was real.

The front clip redirects instead of punishing and it fits a husky's wide chest well. Still working on the training side but this helped immediately.
image_briefs: Husky wearing a well-fitted front-clip harness standing on a sidewalk, leash loose, looking up at owner

authors.voice_note: Second goldendoodle, very experienced, slightly tired of the 'they don't shed' conversation. Knows the grooming reality inside and out and is helpful about it without being preachy.
Got Waffles for allergy reasons, still figuring out the reality gap. Cheerful and honest about what he didn't expect.
Mid-level owner, Clementine is 3, dealing with the jumping and energy that hasn't fully settled. Curious and slightly frustrated in a fond way.
SD local, mini goldendoodle, very active. Brief and practical. Takes Ziggy everywhere and knows the breed well.
Social, loves dog parks, Otto is a standard goldendoodle who has zero concept of personal space. Jess is working on it with humor.
First-time dog owner, Hazel is 10 months, learning everything from scratch. Warm, earnest, asking the beginner questions others are too proud to ask.

posts.title: Got a goldendoodle because my wife is allergic. Update:
posts.content_text:
Waffles sheds. Not like a lab sheds, not like a husky sheds — but he sheds. There is hair on things. My wife's allergist says some people do fine with doodles and some don't, and ours is firmly in the 'don't' camp. She takes an antihistamine now.

Waffles doesn't know any of this and is doing great.

posts.title: How often is everyone actually taking their doodle to the groomer?
posts.content_text:
Fig needs a full groom every 6–8 weeks or she starts matting at the armpits and behind her ears. At $100+ a session that's a real line item. I've been trying to do maintenance brushing between appointments but I can never quite stay ahead of it.

How often are people going, and is anyone doing meaningful upkeep at home to stretch the time between visits?

posts.title: Ziggy at Dog Beach is exactly what you'd expect
posts.content_text:
He swam until I made him stop. Found three other doodles and they formed some kind of pack. Rolled in something unidentified near the rocks. Ate half a sandcastle a kid had abandoned nearby.

Mini doodles are deceptively athletic. He was out there with dogs twice his size and kept up the whole time.
image_briefs: Small golden doodle mid-run on a sandy beach, coat wet from ocean water, mouth wide open in pure joy

posts.title: Running Otto before guests arrive cut the jumping way down
posts.content_text:
Otto is a big dog with no concept of personal space. We tried every training approach for the jumping and the thing that helped most was just running him before people arrive. Twenty minutes of fetch right before and his greeting energy drops way down.

Training still matters but expecting impulse control on a full tank was not working for us.

posts.title: Clementine jumps on everyone and I cannot get it to stop
posts.content_text:
She is three years old and she still launches herself at any human who enters the house. Kids, elderly guests, the delivery driver — doesn't matter. Once she's through the initial greeting she's fine, but that first moment is a lot.

I've done 'off,' I've done turn your back, I've done treats for four paws on the floor. It works in practice sessions and falls apart the moment she gets excited. What finally stuck for people?

posts.title: Check your doodle's ears every week
posts.content_text:
Waffles has had two ear infections this year. Vet explained that the wavy coat combined with floppy ears creates a warm, moist environment that bacteria like. Weekly checks and keeping the hair around the ear canal trimmed has made a real difference.

Not a fun task but it's faster than three rounds of antibiotics.
image_briefs: Close-up of a golden doodle's floppy ear being gently lifted for inspection, dog looks relaxed and unbothered

posts.title: What they don't tell you about goldendoodle coats
posts.content_text:
Fig came home looking like a cloud and I thought she was low-maintenance because the breeder said 'minimal shedding.' Two years in: she gets groomed every seven weeks, she mats if I skip three days of brushing, and the groomer charges more than my own haircuts.

She's worth every bit of it. But I was not prepared for this.
image_briefs: Fluffy golden doodle sitting on a grooming table mid-haircut, coat partially trimmed, looking patient

posts.title: First dog ever – how do I know if I should hire a trainer?
posts.content_text:
Hazel is 10 months and I'm a first-time dog owner. She's sweet but very distracted on walks, pulls a lot, and completely ignores me if she spots something interesting. I've watched videos, tried treats, and I feel like I'm doing something wrong.

At what point do you stop trying to figure it out yourself and hire someone? I don't want to wait too long if I'm reinforcing bad habits.

posts.title: Mental work tires this breed out faster than running does
posts.content_text:
Clementine runs for an hour and still wants to wrestle. Clementine does 20 minutes of nose work and is genuinely done. The poodle brain in a doodle needs actual jobs.

Scatter feeding in the yard, sniff walks where you let them lead, short training sessions — any of these burn more real energy than fetch. We do 15 minutes of scatter feeding most mornings and it sets a better tone for the whole day.

posts.title: Otto doesn't understand that not everyone wants him in their face
posts.content_text:
He approaches strangers like he's been personally invited. Most people love it. Some people don't, and those interactions get awkward fast. Is there a way to teach a dog to read the room, or is this just goldendoodle energy that I need to manage by positioning?

Open to any approaches people have tried.

posts.title: Mini doodle beach morning – OB Dog Beach Saturday
posts.content_text:
Taking Ziggy to OB Dog Beach this Saturday around 8am. He's great with other dogs, especially similarly-sized ones. If you have a mini or medium doodle and want to meet up, we'll be near the south end.

Low key, no agenda, just a beach morning.

posts.title: Slicker brush then metal comb – in that order, every time
posts.content_text:
The routine that finally stopped the mat cycle: slicker brush first to detangle, then a metal comb through every section to find anything you missed. Bathing a matted coat just tightens the knots, so this has to happen before the bath.

If you are not finishing with a metal comb, you are probably leaving hidden mats behind. Takes about 20 minutes and has saved us several emergency grooming visits.

posts.title: At what age did your doodle actually calm down?
posts.content_text:
People kept telling me Clementine would mellow when she turned 2. Then 3. She's 3. Still a tornado. I love her but she is exhausting in a very specific way.

Is there a typical age range where goldendoodles settle or is it just individual? I'm not looking for a fix, just curious what other people's timelines looked like.

posts.title: Doodle playdate at Morley Field – Sunday morning
posts.content_text:
Bringing Otto to Morley Field Sunday around 9am. He's big, friendly, and vaccinated. Looking for other social dogs — doodles, labs, friendly mixes welcome. The off-leash area there is usually pretty relaxed on Sunday mornings.

Comment or DM if you're planning to come.
image_briefs: Golden doodle mid-leap at a grassy off-leash park, chasing another dog, big goofy open-mouth expression

posts.title: Hazel's first groom came back looking uneven – is this within normal range?
posts.content_text:
Took Hazel for her first professional groom last week. She came back looking... uneven. One side is shorter than the other and around her face it looks patchy. I don't have a reference point since this is my first dog and first groom.

Posting a photo — is this within normal range or should I find someone new?
image_briefs: Golden doodle with an uneven post-grooming haircut, coat noticeably different lengths on each side, owner's hand gently holding her face for the photo

authors.voice_note: Third golden, experienced and warm, SD local. Knows the breed deeply and cares about long-term health. Practical and affectionate in tone.
Newer owner, Humphrey is 1 year old. Dealing with mouthiness and outdoor distraction. Good energy, honest about what isn't working.
Works from home, Oatmeal is 5 and settled. Observational and warm. Writes about the quiet, funny daily moments of golden ownership.
Chester is 8, starting to slow down. Sam writes about him with a lot of affection and a little quiet heaviness. Experienced, thoughtful, not looking for advice — sharing.
SD local, beach-oriented, takes Sunny to Dog Beach regularly. Social and practical. Knows Dog Beach dynamics well.
New owner, Wellington is a 4-month-old puppy. Excited and slightly overwhelmed. Asks the questions every new puppy owner has.

posts.title: Biscoff found the garden and carried a zucchini for 20 minutes
posts.content_text:
He pulled a zucchini out of the raised bed, carried it around the yard, brought it to me, then took it back. Just walked around with it, gentle as anything. Didn't chew it, didn't destroy it. Just had it.

He is exactly the dog this breed is supposed to be and moments like this remind me why I keep getting goldens.
image_briefs: Golden Retriever proudly carrying a large zucchini in his mouth across a backyard, tail mid-wag, looking very pleased

posts.title: Wellington's fur is still really light at 4 months – does it change?
posts.content_text:
Wellington is 4 months and his coat is very fluffy and almost cream-colored. I was expecting more gold. The breeder said the color would deepen but I'm curious when that usually happens and whether some just stay light.

Posting a photo because he is very cute and I also need some reassurance.
image_briefs: Cream-colored golden retriever puppy with very fluffy cottony coat, sitting on grass looking up at camera

posts.title: Flirt pole before walks cut our leash pulling in half
posts.content_text:
Humphrey was dragging me on every single walk. Tried gentle leaders, treat-based loose leash work, management tools — nothing stuck. Someone suggested burning energy with a flirt pole for 15 minutes first. First week, pulling dropped by half.

Not a replacement for training but if your dog is already over-threshold before you even start, exercise first. Posting a photo of what a post-flirt-pole golden actually looks like.
image_briefs: Golden Retriever lying flat on a lawn completely wiped out but happy, panting with eyes soft and half-closed

posts.title: Golden meetup at Dog Beach – Saturday morning
posts.content_text:
Taking Sunny to Dog Beach Saturday around 8am. She does especially well with her own breed so would love to meet other golden owners. We'll be near the north end.

Come say hi.
image_briefs: Two golden retrievers running together on an off-leash beach, both mid-sprint, ocean and morning light in background

posts.title: Oatmeal has a very specific way of placing her orders
posts.content_text:
She puts her paw on my knee. Not urgently — very gently, like she's placing an order. If I don't respond she lifts it and replaces it, same spot, same pressure. This happens maybe 15 times a day.

She has fully figured out that it works and she is exploiting it without any shame.
image_briefs: Golden Retriever with her paw gently resting on a person's knee, looking up with soft patient eyes

posts.title: Chester is 8 now and everything is a little slower
posts.content_text:
We still do our walks but they're shorter. He still wants to fetch but stops sooner. He sleeps more and sometimes I hear him creak getting up from his bed in the morning. The vet says he's doing well for his age.

This is the part of having a golden that nobody talks about enough. They're with you for maybe 10–12 years and the last few look different than the first. I'm grateful for every single one.

posts.title: Golden owners – are you doing any proactive cancer screening?
posts.content_text:
Biscoff is 5 and I've been reading more about the elevated cancer rates in goldens, especially American-bred lines. My vet mentioned it but didn't go deep. I'm wondering if people are doing anything beyond a standard yearly exam — blood panels, imaging, anything.

Or is it mostly a wait-and-watch approach? I'd rather be proactive if there's something reasonable to do.

posts.title: Humphrey is 9 months and still mouthing everything – did I miss a window?
posts.content_text:
Not hard biting, no breaking skin, but constant mouthing of hands, arms, clothing, leashes. I thought this settled down around 6 months. We've done bite inhibition work, lots of redirection, timeouts. He's 9 months now and still at it.

Is this normal for goldens or did I miss a window somewhere?

posts.title: Does your golden hit a wall at Dog Beach and get weird?
posts.content_text: Sunny is great at Dog Beach but around the 90-minute mark she starts getting nippy and over-stimulated. Like she can't read social cues anymore. I've started leaving before she gets there but I'm curious if others notice this and whether there's anything to do about it besides just managing the duration.

posts.title: Use mealtimes as quick training sessions – golden brains like it
posts.content_text:
Oatmeal would work for food indefinitely. I started doing 2–3 minutes of obedience work before every meal — sits, downs, place, whatever we're working on. By the time the bowl lands, she's focused and has actually earned it.

Small thing but it's kept her sharp at 5 without needing long formal sessions.

posts.title: Teach 'go get your toy' before people arrive at the door
posts.content_text:
Biscoff needs something in his mouth at greetings or he mouths hands and jumps. The fix that actually worked: teaching him to find a toy when the doorbell rings. He sprints to get it, comes back to show the guest, and everyone's happy.

This is my third golden and the best doorbell protocol I've found. Redirect the retrieve instinct instead of fighting it.

posts.title: At what point did your golden actually start paying attention outside?
posts.content_text:
Humphrey listens well inside. Outside — other dogs, smells, people walking past — he completely checks out. I know he understands the commands because he does them at home without any issue.

I am not giving up, just trying to figure out when this typically clicks. Was there a specific age or training milestone that marked the shift for you?

posts.title: Managing hip stiffness in an older golden – what's actually helped?
posts.content_text:
Chester has started showing stiffness after rest, especially in his hips and back legs. He's on joint supplements and we've slowed down the mileage. Looking for what else people have found useful — specific supplements, changes to sleep setup, swimming, anything.

He's 8, otherwise healthy. Just want to keep him comfortable.

posts.title: Leave before the meltdown, not during it
posts.content_text:
Staying at Dog Beach until Sunny's behavior fell apart meant she went home overstimulated and it bled into the next day. Now I leave when things are still going well — she is having fun but not frantic. Shorter visits with better endings made a real difference for us.

Leaving when it still feels early is basically the whole strategy.

posts.title: Puppy playdate for goldens or friendly mixes – North Park
posts.content_text:
Wellington needs friends. He's 4 months, fully vaccinated, and absolutely full of it. Looking for other puppy owners or calm adult dogs for a backyard playdate in North Park. Nothing formal — just some socialization time.

DM if interested.

authors.voice_note: Adopted Fennel from a rescue in Chula Vista, warm and community-oriented without being preachy. Fennel is some kind of hound-lab-boxer mix. Paloma advocates for rescues but keeps it grounded.
Got a DNA test that completely upended what he thought Desi was. Casual, funny, slightly fascinated by the whole thing.
Third rescue, experienced and practical. Huck is a medium-energy mystery mix. Rosie knows how to fill in the blanks on a dog with no history.
First dog, adopted Maple as an adult. Earnest and thoughtful about navigating unknowns. Maple was listed as lab mix and is clearly not a lab.
Longtime rescue advocate, Beets was a stray for two years before the shelter. Direct and warm. Writes about rescue timelines honestly.
SD local, outdoorsy, Gravel is his trail and walking companion. Brief and practical. Knows what works for his specific dog.
Cricket is a 12-lb chihuahua terrier mix who carries herself like she's enormous. Leila writes about her with bemused affection.

posts.title: One year with Fennel
posts.content_text:
We adopted her from a rescue in Chula Vista 12 months ago. I remember thinking she was too shut down and I'd made a wrong decision — she barely made eye contact for the first two weeks. Now she sleeps on our bed, greets us at the door, and has strong opinions about when it's time for her walk.

Rescue timelines are not linear and I'm grateful we gave her the time she needed.
image_briefs: Medium-sized mixed breed dog lying comfortably on a couch, looking directly at camera with relaxed soft eyes

posts.title: Cricket is 12 lbs and genuinely believes she can take down anything
posts.content_text:
She will walk up to a Rottweiler like she has been personally invited to end him. She is some kind of chihuahua terrier mix and she is completely immune to consequences.

She is not aggressive. Just has a self-image that does not match her actual size at all. Small dog confidence issue or socialization gap? Posting a photo for scale.
image_briefs: Tiny mixed breed dog standing chest-puffed next to a much larger dog, completely unbothered and looking up at it

posts.title: Maple was listed as 'lab mix' – she is definitely not a lab
posts.content_text:
She weighs 27 lbs, has a long wiry coat, and can vertical jump about four feet from a standstill. The shelter listed her as lab mix because she was black. She is not a lab.

What breed label did your rescue have that turned out to be completely off?

posts.title: Mixed breed meetup at Balboa Park – Saturday morning
posts.content_text:
Bringing Huck to Balboa Park Saturday around 8:30am near the dog run on the west side. Open to all mixed breeds, rescues, and genuine mystery dogs. No pressure, no agenda, just a walk with some well-meaning mutts.

Hit me up if you're coming.

posts.title: Beets was a stray for two years before the rescue found her
posts.content_text:
We were told this upfront. She'd survived on her own for two years somewhere in East County before animal control brought her in. She knew how to find food, stay hidden, and not trust anyone.

She's been with us five months. Last week she put her head in my lap for the first time and stayed there for 20 minutes. That's what five months of patience looks like.

posts.title: Figure out your specific dog's version of exercise, not the breed's
posts.content_text:
Gravel looks like he should be a distance runner — he has the build and the energy. Turned out he has terrier in him and prefers short bursts with a lot of sniffing over sustained endurance. I was pushing him on long hikes and he was tolerating it, not thriving.

Shorter walks with more stops turned out to be his thing. Mixed breeds especially don't always follow the template.

posts.title: Did your dog's DNA results change how you interact with them?
posts.content_text:
Fennel's results came back as 40% hound, 25% lab, 20% boxer, 15% pit. I thought she was just a generic lab mix. Now I'm wondering if her nose-first approach to everything is the hound in her and whether I should be incorporating more scent work.

Did knowing the breakdown change anything practical for you, or did you just sort of move on?

posts.title: Desi does this whole food coverage ritual before every meal and I have no explanation
posts.content_text:
She pushes her food around with her nose, covers it with an invisible blanket, then eats. Every single meal. She is not food insecure — finishes everything every time. Just has to complete the ritual first.

Did your dog's DNA results explain any specific behaviors like this? Or did it just raise more questions than it answered?

posts.title: Write down everything you notice in the first two weeks – it actually pays off
posts.content_text:
With a rescue you do not have a history. What you can do is observe and document: what triggers alertness, what causes hesitation, what they love, what shuts them down. After two weeks I had a page of notes on Huck that was more useful than anything the shelter had told me.

Vets and trainers work a lot better with specifics. It helps them calibrate advice instead of just guessing.
image_briefs: Mixed breed dog sitting calmly on a rug next to a small notebook, looking up attentively at the camera

posts.title: Three months in and Beets still seems on edge – what did your timeline actually look like?
posts.content_text:
Beets has been with us three months and she is better — but she still startles at sounds, cannot settle unless she can see the door, and gets anxious when we leave. I know rescue timelines vary a lot.

What did three months, six months, a year look like for yours? Just trying to get a realistic picture of what is ahead.

posts.title: Questions to ask a rescue before adoption day
posts.content_text:
The intake questions that actually helped us: how does the dog react to strangers at home vs. on walks, any known triggers, any history of food guarding, how did previous placements go. Not every rescue has all this information but asking surfaces whatever they do know.

We learned Fennel had been returned once before. That explained her first two weeks completely.

posts.title: Schedule a full vet workup in the first month, before you need it
posts.content_text:
We had Maple for three weeks before I finally booked her first full exam. She came with a health certificate from the rescue but I wanted a baseline from a vet starting fresh. Caught a mild heart murmur that's been on our radar ever since — not urgent but worth knowing.

Rescues do their best but they're not your regular vet. Get an independent baseline early.

posts.title: Does your dog do things that contradict what the DNA says?
posts.content_text:
Huck tested as 50% retriever and has absolutely no interest in fetching. None. Won't chase a ball, won't pick up a stick. Loves to swim but only in circles, never toward anything.

What do your mixed breeds do that completely contradicts what their genetics would predict?

posts.title: Desi's DNA results: not even close to what I expected
posts.content_text:
I was told she was a shepherd mix. She looked like a shepherd mix — medium build, upright ears, tan markings. DNA results: 38% chow chow, 30% basenji, 22% Australian cattle dog, 10% terrier. Zero shepherd. Not a trace.

I spent two years guessing completely wrong. She is now a totally different dog in my head, though the exact same dog in real life, which is the part that matters.
image_briefs: Mixed breed dog with upright ears and tan markings sitting alertly on a park bench, looking off to the side

posts.title: Mixed breed walk at Sunset Cliffs – Sunday evening
posts.content_text:
Taking Gravel out to Sunset Cliffs this Sunday around 6pm, walking the path north toward Ocean Beach. Great views, not too hot at that hour, good smells.

Usually about 45 minutes. If you have a mix of any kind and want company, we'll be at the parking area off Sunset Cliffs Blvd.
image_briefs: Mixed breed dog on a coastal path overlooking the Pacific at golden hour, leash on, owner's feet visible beside them

authors.voice_note: Second pug, very matter-of-fact about the breed's health realities. Not alarmist, just informed. Benny is three and managed well.
First pug, Tulip is a black pug. Marco is charmed and learning quickly. Writes with genuine curiosity about breed-specific things he didn't expect.
Barnaby is seven, Stephanie is experienced with senior pug management. Warm and slightly anxious about his health, but she knows the breed well.
New pug owner, Noodles is a puppy, David is completely besotted. Asks the alarmed beginner questions with full enthusiasm.
Mid-level owner, Figgy is 3, San Diego local. Practical about managing pug life in a warm climate. Takes Figgy to shaded parks early in the morning.
Previous dog was a 70-lb lab. Now has a pug. The adjustment has been genuinely funny. He's learned to appreciate Wallace on Wallace's own terms.

posts.title: I recorded Benny sleeping and regret nothing
posts.content_text:
Sent the video to my sister thinking she'd find the snoring cute. She called me and asked if I had a sleep apnea machine running. I had to explain it was a 14-lb dog.

Benny sleeps on his back with his tongue slightly out and breathes like a vintage coffeemaker. I have never loved anything more.
image_briefs: Pug sleeping on his back on a couch cushion, tongue slightly out, completely relaxed and looking absurd

posts.title: Coming from a lab, owning a pug is a different universe
posts.content_text:
My last dog was a 70-lb lab who would run five miles without giving it a second thought. Wallace will walk to the end of the block, decide the world is satisfactory, and want to go back inside. He snores. He requires face maintenance. He overheats if I look at him wrong.

I genuinely did not understand this breed before I had one. He is also the funniest animal I've ever lived with.
image_briefs: Pug sitting on a front doorstep with an extremely serious expression, looking like he has already decided the outside world is not worth it

posts.title: Noodles does this alarming gasping thing – is this reverse sneezing?
posts.content_text:
About once a day she goes into an episode where she inhales rapidly and makes a honking sound. Lasts 20–30 seconds and then she's completely fine. First time it happened I genuinely thought something was wrong.

Vet said probably reverse sneezing. Does it stop as they get older? Is there anything that helps during the episode?

posts.title: Pug meetup at Morley Field – shaded end, Sunday morning
posts.content_text:
Bringing Figgy to Morley Field Sunday around 7:30am — the shaded end near the trees, before it gets warm. She does well with other small dogs. If you have a pug or brachycephalic breed, come say hi. We'll keep it short and in the shade.

DM or comment if you're coming.
image_briefs: Pug sitting in dappled shade on a grassy park lawn, looking toward camera, trees visible in the background

posts.title: Tulip's eye keeps getting red – what's normal for this breed?
posts.content_text:
She's a black pug and her eyes seem to water more than I expected. Sometimes the inner corner gets red, sometimes there's brownish discharge in the morning. My vet said it's normal for the breed but also said to watch it.

What am I watching for? When does it cross from 'pug thing' to 'actual problem'?

posts.title: Pugs respond to your energy as much as to treats
posts.content_text:
Barnaby is seven and I still use treats, but I've found he responds just as strongly to genuine enthusiasm. If I get excited about something he did right, he repeats it. Pugs track your emotional state closely.

At this point he almost prefers the approval, especially mid-walk when he's not that food-motivated. Worth trying if treats aren't landing.

posts.title: Clean the face folds every day and actually get in there
posts.content_text:
Benny gets fold dermatitis if I skip two days. The routine that finally works for us: dry cotton round twice a day, nothing else. The area has to be completely dry after — moisture is what causes the irritation and odor.

Took me a while to get consistent with it but it has made a real difference. Way easier than dealing with a vet appointment for something preventable.
image_briefs: Close-up of a pug's facial wrinkle being gently wiped with a cotton pad, dog sitting still and cooperative

posts.title: Figgy invented a sock game and I'm just a participant now
posts.content_text:
She brings me a sock. I don't do anything with it. She takes it back. She brings it back. This goes on for 10–15 minutes most evenings. I'm not sure if it's a fetch attempt, a ritual, or some kind of test.

She's been doing this for two years. I think this is just our thing.

posts.title: How to stop a reverse sneezing episode quickly
posts.content_text:
Our vet showed us two things: gently cover the nostrils for 2–3 seconds to trigger a swallow reflex, or lightly massage the throat. Both usually end the episode faster than waiting it out.

Doesn't prevent them but it helps me stay calm and it helps her reset quicker. Becomes muscle memory after a few weeks.

posts.title: keeping a cloth by Tulip's water bowl cleared up a recurring skin issue
posts.content_text:
Tulip gets water all over her face wrinkles when she drinks. Leave it wet and the fold area starts getting irritated within a day or two. Just keeping a small cloth next to her bowl for a quick dry-off after she drinks fixed it.

One of those small habits that just works. Wish I had landed on it sooner.

posts.title: When did you decide to do the BOAS surgery?
posts.content_text:
Benny has elongated soft palate and slightly stenotic nares. He manages okay — not great, but okay. He's three. My vet says surgery would meaningfully improve his quality of life and he's a good candidate, but it's not urgent yet.

For those who've done it: what was the tipping point? And did you see a real difference afterward?

posts.title: Figgy can barely handle 15 minutes in June – what does your summer routine look like?
posts.content_text:
We're in San Diego and June through September genuinely limits what Figgy can do. She manages maybe 15 minutes at 6am before she's panting heavily. I've shifted to two short walks on the cooler ends of the day instead of one mid-day walk.

Is anyone doing anything creative to keep their pug active in summer without the heat risk?

posts.title: Barnaby's breathing sounds different lately – when do you call the vet?
posts.content_text:
He has always been a noisy breather — snorts, gurgles, the whole pug soundtrack. But something has sounded different these past few weeks. The honking is happening more often and at rest, not just when he is excited or warm. He is seven.

I can never quite tell with pugs what is normal-for-them versus worth a call. How do you judge that line?

posts.title: Casual pug walk around South Park – Sunday morning
posts.content_text:
Taking Wallace on a shaded neighborhood walk around South Park this Sunday around 7am. Quiet streets, lots of trees, good for brachycephalic dogs that need to stay cool.

If you have a pug or short-snout breed and want company, come find us. Nothing structured.

posts.title: Can anyone help me identify this eye thing on Barnaby?
posts.content_text:
There's a small pink growth in the inner corner of his eye. It's been there about a week and isn't getting bigger but it's definitely new. I know pug eyes are delicate so I'm not inclined to wait on this.

Posting a photo. Has anyone seen this before? Cherry eye or something else?
image_briefs: Close-up of a pug's face with a small pink protrusion visible in the inner corner of the eye, dog looking straight at camera

authors.voice_note: Second frenchie, very practical about the health realities. Not alarmist, just consistent. Knows what happens when you skip maintenance and does not skip maintenance.
First frenchie, Potato is 8 months, Ryan is charmed and slightly bewildered. Has a good sense of humor about the breed's opinions on everything.
Mid-level owner, Gus is 3, dealing with ongoing skin allergies. Thoughtful and resourceful. Has tried a lot of things and keeps track of what worked.
SD local, active lifestyle, has learned to work with Millie's limits rather than against them. Practical and brief.
Second frenchie, experienced, warm and helpful. Knows the breed well and shares things she wishes she'd known earlier.
Previous dog was a bigger, more compliant breed. The adjustment to Bertie's sense of self-governance has been funny and ongoing.

posts.title: Potato screams when I pick him up – is this a frenchie thing?
posts.content_text:
I reach for him in a completely normal way and he makes a sound like I'm ending him. No injury, vet says he's fine, the drama is just intense. I've asked other frenchie owners and they all immediately started laughing.

Does this ever stop? Or do I just have a theatrical dog?

posts.title: Archie versus the 10-minute morning walk
posts.content_text:
The route to the park is 10 minutes. Archie is done at minute six. Not tired — just done. He sits down on the sidewalk and looks at me like I've made a serious error in judgment. We turn around. Every day.

He is four years old and has the constitution of a Victorian convalescent. I love him completely.
image_briefs: French Bulldog sitting stubbornly on a sidewalk mid-walk, looking up at owner with complete indignation

posts.title: Frenchie beach morning at Coronado – Sunday 7:30am
posts.content_text:
Taking Millie to Coronado Dog Beach this Sunday morning around 7:30. Early enough that the sand is cool and it's not crowded. She mostly sits near the water and judges other dogs but she loves the outing.

If you have a frenchie or brachycephalic breed and want to meet up, we'll be near the south end.
image_briefs: French Bulldog sitting on wet sand at a beach in early morning light, looking out toward the water with dignified curiosity

posts.title: Gus decided his dog bed was for other dogs
posts.content_text:
Gus had a perfectly good dog bed. He used it twice. Since then his position has been that his bed is the left couch cushion, specifically with the throw pillow. The dog bed sits in the corner looking dejected.

Every morning he stands at the couch and waits to be lifted up, even though I've watched him make the jump alone at least a dozen times. The couch is his and I am staff.
image_briefs: French Bulldog sitting possessively on a couch cushion next to a throw pillow, looking completely settled and unbothered

posts.title: how do you actually get a frenchie to do what you want
posts.content_text:
My previous dog was a lab. The lab listened. Bertie listens to Bertie. He is sweet, affectionate, never aggressive — just deeply unimpressed by authority. Treats help but only when he feels like it.

How do frenchie owners actually approach training with a dog who seems to run a constant calculation on whether compliance is worth the effort?

posts.title: Potato has a whole morning check-in ritual and it is completely non-negotiable
posts.content_text:
He will not acknowledge that it is daytime until I have sat next to him on the couch for at least five minutes. Not petting him. Just present. If I skip it he stares at me until I comply.

He started this around five months old and it has only become more established. Does anyone else have this?
image_briefs: French Bulldog sitting on a couch with a very serious unblinking stare aimed directly at the camera, clearly waiting for something

posts.title: frenchie bat ears – the weekly routine I wish I had started on day one
posts.content_text:
Archie gets buildup fast because of how his ears sit. They trap dust, debris, wax. Quick wipe with a dry cotton ball once a week, takes about two minutes. My first frenchie had two ear infections before I figured this out.

Once it is part of the routine it takes almost no time. Before it is in the routine it turns into a vet bill.

posts.title: Pascal found a bug and I thought something was wrong with him
posts.content_text:
He was frozen in the middle of the living room, staring at something on the floor. Completely rigid, wouldn't respond to his name. I was convinced something was neurologically wrong. I got close and it was a small beetle, also frozen, possibly terrified.

They stared at each other for four minutes. The beetle eventually walked away. Pascal looked at me with what I can only describe as relief.

posts.title: Slow feeder bowl helped Potato's gas situation more than anything else
posts.content_text:
Potato was eating so fast he was inhaling air and the results were impressive in the worst way. Switched to a slow feeder bowl and within a week the constant air-swallowing farts dropped a lot. He still has some frenchie-standard digestive opinions but the volume is more manageable.

Worth trying before you go down a food allergy investigation path.

posts.title: Managing frenchie skin allergies without a full elimination diet – possible?
posts.content_text:
Gus has had itchy paws, a red belly, and occasional hot spots since he was about 18 months. Vet confirmed environmental and possibly food allergies. We've done one elimination diet and it helped somewhat but was never fully conclusive.

Are people managing this with supplements, specific proteins, or something else? I feel like I've been in a permanent trial-and-error loop for a year.

posts.title: Anyone switched to limited ingredient food for frenchie skin issues?
posts.content_text:
Archie has had skin sensitivity since he was young — red patches between his toes, occasional itching. Vet has suggested a limited ingredient or hydrolyzed protein diet to rule out food allergies. Has anyone gone through this process with a frenchie?

How long before you saw a difference and what did you land on?

posts.title: Two short walks at the cool ends of the day – frenchies don't do midday heat
posts.content_text:
Millie can handle about 20–25 minutes in decent weather. In SD summers she can barely handle 15 if it's past 8am. Two short walks at dawn and dusk has been our routine for two years and it works.

Don't let anyone make you feel like you're under-exercising them. Frenchies genuinely don't need or want a five-mile run.

posts.title: The tail pocket is easy to forget and hard to ignore when you do
posts.content_text:
Pascal has a tight screw tail and the fold underneath gets red and irritated if I skip cleaning it. I did not even know frenchies had this until his first groomer mentioned it. Small fold right under the tail that traps moisture — clean it like a face fold, dry cotton, fully dried after.

Easy thing to miss, annoying when you do. Once a week works well for us.
image_briefs: French Bulldog tail area showing the characteristic screw tail and the fold beneath it, dog relaxed on a grooming mat

posts.title: Frenchie meetup at Grape Street Dog Park – Saturday morning
posts.content_text:
Taking Bertie to Grape Street Saturday around 8am. He's social with other small dogs and does well in the small dog area. Looking for other frenchie or flat-face breed owners to meet up with.

Comment if you're coming.

posts.title: Is anyone proactively screening their frenchie for IVDD?
posts.content_text:
Pascal is two and I've been reading that frenchies are one of the higher-risk chondrodystrophic breeds for spinal issues. Our vet hasn't brought it up on their own. I'm wondering if there's a standard screening protocol people do or if it's mostly monitor-for-symptoms until something happens.

I know it's more associated with dachshunds but frenchies are on the list too and I want to be thoughtful about it.

authors.voice_note: Third pit bull, experienced and knowledgeable. Advocates for the breed through quality ownership rather than rhetoric. Knows the challenges and navigates them practically.
Ruthie is a rescue, 2 years in, working through leash reactivity. Patient and methodical. Approaches the work like a slow project he's committed to.
Newer owner, Peanut is a puppy, SD local. Excited about early socialization and doing everything right from the start.
Experienced, runs structured training with Clyde. Practical, confident, brief. Believes in building the dog's skill set systematically.
Works from home, Margot is her second pit, SD local. Thoughtful and warm. Writes about Margot with a lot of quiet pride.
Navigating breed-restricted housing as a renter. Practical about the reality without being bitter about it. Bruno is an exceptionally well-behaved dog.
Casual but consistent owner, Hank is a brindle pit who's been with her four years. Writes warmly and a little wryly about life with him.

posts.title: People cross the street when they see Hank and I'm not sure how to feel about it
posts.content_text:
Hank is a 55-lb brindle pit who is afraid of plastic bags and sleeps with a stuffed duck. He has never once done anything threatening in four years. But I watch people cross the street when we're coming.

Is this something you get used to? I'm not angry, it's just a little deflating sometimes. Attaching a photo so you can see what kind of dangerous animal we're dealing with.
image_briefs: Brindle pit bull sitting on a sidewalk next to a small stuffed animal toy, looking sweet and docile, completely harmless energy

posts.title: Duke passed his Canine Good Citizen test today
posts.content_text:
Three months of consistent work and he passed every element. The evaluator mentioned afterward that he was one of the calmest dogs she'd tested in a while. I'm not going to pretend I wasn't emotional.

For anyone wondering if the work is worth it — it is. Whatever breed you have. The CGC process teaches you as much as it teaches the dog.
image_briefs: Pit bull sitting proudly outdoors wearing a bandana, CGC ribbon visible near his collar, looking calm and composed

posts.title: Peanut met a cat today and handled it better than expected
posts.content_text:
My neighbor has an outdoor cat with zero fear of dogs. They met through the fence — Peanut went still, did a slow sniff, then tried a play bow. The cat hissed and walked off. Peanut looked confused but stayed calm and didn't amp up about it.

I know prey drive can develop later but this was a good first data point. Starting careful introductions early feels like the right move.
image_briefs: Pit bull puppy looking through a wooden fence at a cat on the other side, tail up in curiosity, soft gentle posture

posts.title: The front-clip harness made Ruthie's walks actually manageable
posts.content_text:
Ruthie is 60 lbs and was pulling me all over the place before we switched to a front-clip harness. The redirect when she pulls makes walks manageable while we keep working on training. It is not a substitute for the training work but it keeps both of us safe in the meantime.

We use the Ruffwear Front Range. Holds up well and fits the wider chest.
image_briefs: Pit bull wearing a well-fitted front-clip harness on a suburban sidewalk, looking up at owner with a loose relaxed leash

posts.title: How do you handle it when another dog escalates toward yours?
posts.content_text:
Duke is solid — trained, predictable, not the problem. The problem is other dogs going after him and owners who then look at me like it was my dog's fault. He holds it together but I'm always wondering if I'm managing these situations correctly.

What's your approach when your pit is behaving and the other dog isn't?

posts.title: Structured pit meet at Grape Street – Sunday 8am
posts.content_text:
Taking Clyde to Grape Street Dog Park this Sunday morning. Looking for other pit or similar-breed owners who are actively managing their dogs — not looking for chaos, looking for a casual meet where owners are paying attention.

DM me if you're interested so we can coordinate slightly before showing up.
image_briefs: Pit bull sitting calmly on a park path next to owner's feet, looking composed and relaxed, green park background

posts.title: Duke's good manners have done more for the breed than anything I could say
posts.content_text:
This is my third pit and what I have found is that the most practical thing I can do is just have a dog who holds a sit, walks nicely on leash, and has reliable recall. Duke gets compliments from people who admit they were nervous when they first saw him. That shift happens because he gives them nothing to be nervous about.

The basics matter a lot with this breed. Not as a statement — just because a well-trained dog is a well-trained dog, and people notice.

posts.title: How much socialization is the right amount for a pit puppy?
posts.content_text:
Peanut is 14 weeks and I want to socialize her as much as possible but I've also read that overstimulation at this age can backfire. Dog parks, puppy classes, street walks — trying to figure out the right pace for a breed where early socialization really matters.

What did your schedule look like at this age?

posts.title: Ruthie has leash reactivity toward other dogs – where do I start?
posts.content_text:
She's completely fine with other dogs in person — she's met plenty and plays well. But on leash with a dog approaching, she loses it. Lunging, barking, can't refocus until the other dog has passed. She's 60 lbs and it's a lot to manage.

Is this leash frustration or true reactivity? And what has actually worked for people starting from this point?

posts.title: Margot is a better judge of people than I am
posts.content_text:
She warms up to almost everyone within five minutes. But there have been two people in four years where she stayed distant, stayed between me and them, and just would not let go of something I could not identify. No barking, no growling — just would not relax.

Both times my gut agreed with her. I have stopped second-guessing it.

posts.title: spent the first two months on impulse control before anything else – worth it
posts.content_text:
Before working on anything specific, I spent two months on just impulse control with Clyde: leave it, wait at thresholds, no forward movement on leash unless released. Sounds basic but a pit with solid impulse control handles real-world situations very differently.

If I was starting over with a new pit, this is where the first couple of months would go.

posts.title: How do you find pit-friendly rental housing in San Diego?
posts.content_text:
Bruno has a CGC title. He has never been in any kind of trouble. We've been turned down by three apartments this year because of his breed.

Does anyone have actual strategies that work — specific property management companies, how to present your dog during the application process, anything practical? I'm not looking for validation, I'm looking for what's actually worked.

posts.title: Clyde is dog-selective – how do you handle group settings?
posts.content_text:
He likes some dogs and is indifferent to others. The ones he's indifferent to he mostly ignores. Occasionally he'll get tense with a specific dog and I'll pull him out. He's never started anything but I stay aware.

How do people with dog-selective pits handle dog parks or group meetups? Do you skip them or is there a way to make it work?

posts.title: Sunday morning walk at Balboa Park – pit owners welcome
posts.content_text:
I do a regular Sunday walk at Balboa Park with Margot, usually starting around 8am from the 6th Avenue entrance. She's friendly and well-managed. I just like walking with other large dog owners who are invested in their dogs.

If you have a pit or similar and want to join occasionally, let me know.

posts.title: Build a dog resume before apartment hunting
posts.content_text:
After getting rejected twice, I put together a one-page document for Bruno: CGC certification, vet reference, previous landlord reference, a professional photo. Not every landlord cares but it changes the conversation with the ones who might be on the fence.

A few have told us it made a real difference. Takes about an hour to put together and it's the kind of thing that carries forward.

authors.voice_note: Third lab, very practical about weight and food management. Has fought the lab weight battle twice before and won. SD local.
Newer owner, Olive is 18 months and a chaos machine. Chris is funny and honest about what he didn't expect. Still figuring out the training side.
Mid-level owner, Fletcher is 5, they swim at Dog Beach regularly. Warm and active. Has fully accepted that Dog Beach takes the whole morning.
First dog, yellow lab, Dot is 11 months. Earnest and curious. Writes like someone who's doing their best and finding it delightful.
